topic: An athlete doesn't make the team they expected to join
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest. I traced the faded logo on my jersey, the fabric rough beneath my fingertips. Everyone else was laughing, the easy camaraderie of the incoming squad a warm tide I was conspicuously standing outside of. I kept my eyes fixed on the floor, focusing intently on the scuff marks near my worn sneakers, anything to avoid meeting anyone's gaze. When Coach finally called the names, my stomach plummeted. "Next up," he announced, his voice booming, "is Marcus." A wave of heat washed over my face, making my skin prickle. I nodded, a jerky, almost involuntary movement, and shuffled toward the exit, every step feeling weighted with disappointment.

[story 2]

I watched the team huddle from the edge of the bleachers, the vibrant energy of their shared excitement radiating outward like heat. My hands were clammy, gripping the cold metal railing until my knuckles turned white. Every time a teammate clapped, a sharp, unwelcome jolt went through me. I tried to appear casual, pretending to adjust my backpack strap, but I could feel the scrutiny, the way their eyes seemed to linger on me, assessing the gap between who I was and who they needed. When the final roster was posted, the names blurred. My own name wasn't there. A dull ache settled low in my abdomen, a heavy, undeniable weight that made breathing shallow.

[story 3]

"Good luck out there," I managed, the words sounding thin and reedy even to my own ears. I forced a smile, a practiced, brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes. The air in the hallway felt thick, suffocating. I could hear the muffled chatter of the selected players, a sound that seemed to mock my quiet defeat. Walking toward the exit felt like wading through molasses; my shoulders were hunched inward, trying to shrink my frame. When I reached the door, I hesitated, the desire to simply disappear overwhelming. I pulled the door open, stepping out into the bright afternoon sun, feeling utterly exposed and profoundly small.
